news_summary: Significant advances in renewable energy and sustainable practices are underway, with major investments in zero-carbon hydrogen hubs, innovative geothermal power projects, and community-driven climate action initiatives promoting eco-friendly landscaping and water conservation.
news_summary_fr: Des progrès significatifs sont en cours dans le domaine des énergies renouvelables et des pratiques durables, avec des investissements majeurs dans des pôles hydrogène zéro carbone, des projets innovants d'énergie géothermique et des initiatives communautaires en faveur du climat qui encouragent l'aménagement paysager écologique et la conservation de l'eau.
news_summary_es: Se están produciendo avances significativos en materia de energías renovables y prácticas sostenibles, con importantes inversiones en centros de hidrógeno sin emisiones de carbono, proyectos innovadores de energía geotérmica e iniciativas comunitarias de acción climática que promueven el paisajismo ecológico y la conservación del agua.

articles.title: HydrogenXT secures $900 million to launch 10 zero-carbon hydrogen hubs across the US
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM In a sign of how quickly the clean energy landscape is evolving, HydrogenXT has secured a $900 million financing agreement to build an initial fleet of 10 zero-carbon hydrogen production and refueling facilities across the United States. For an industry that, not long ago, was dominated by fossil fuels, […]
The post HydrogenXT secures $900 million to launch 10 zero-carbon hydrogen hubs across the US first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

In a sign of how quickly the clean energy landscape is evolving, HydrogenXT has secured a $900 million financing agreement to build an initial fleet of 10 zero-carbon hydrogen production and refueling facilities across the United States.

For an industry that, not long ago, was dominated by fossil fuels, such large-scale investments in hydrogen infrastructure would have seemed improbable. In 2026, however, billion-dollar clean energy deals are becoming a defining feature of the transition.

The Houston-based company’s agreement with Kell Kapital Partners Limited and a consortium of institutional investors marks a major milestone, not only for HydrogenXT, but for the broader push to decarbonize heavy transport and industry.

Building a network of zero-carbon hydrogen hubs

The financing will fund the engineering, procurement, and construction of ten localized hydrogen plants designed to produce and dispense zero-carbon, fuel-grade hydrogen. The facilities are intended to serve heavy-duty transportation, industrial users, and emerging energy applications that are difficult to electrify.

Hydrogen has gained momentum in recent years because of its versatility. It can power trucks, buses, and industrial processes while emitting little to no carbon at the point of use. As governments and corporations look to cut emissions across supply chains, demand for low-carbon hydrogen is rising.

HydrogenXT’s project reflects that shift. The first facility is planned for Avenal, California, with additional sites targeted along major freight and logistics corridors in California, Oregon, Washington, and North Dakota. These locations were selected strategically. They are close to transportation routes and demand centers where diesel still dominates.

While some communities may question why facilities are concentrated in certain regions, the company’s strategy focuses on areas where hydrogen can displace the most fossil fuel use, particularly in heavy trucking.

How the $900 million deal is structured

Large-scale energy projects rarely move forward without complex financial engineering. HydrogenXT’s $900 million package blends debt and equity financing, structured specifically to support the rollout of its first wave of plants.

The involvement of Kell Kapital Partners Limited, a firm with a strong track record in infrastructure and clean energy, adds credibility to the effort. Institutional backing at this level signals confidence that hydrogen infrastructure can be commercially viable, not just environmentally desirable.

For HydrogenXT, the financing accelerates a transition from concept to construction. Reliable capital allows the company to scale quickly and establish a foothold in what many analysts expect to become a highly competitive hydrogen market.

The technology behind the facilities

HydrogenXT plans to deploy a modular platform that combines steam methane reforming with integrated carbon capture, along with on-site compression, storage, and dispensing systems.

Steam methane reforming has long been used to produce hydrogen, but pairing it with carbon capture technology significantly reduces associated emissions. By integrating compression, storage, and fueling systems directly on site, the facilities are designed to streamline distribution and lower operational costs.

The goal: deliver affordable, zero-carbon hydrogen that can compete with conventional fuels in sectors where battery-electric solutions may not yet be practical.

A broader shift in energy investment

The scale of the financing underscores a broader transformation underway in the energy sector. As carbon reduction targets tighten and climate risks become more apparent, capital is increasingly flowing toward infrastructure that supports low-emission alternatives.

While $900 million may seem like a steep investment, large energy systems have always required substantial upfront funding. Historically, fossil fuel projects attracted similar (if not larger) sums. The difference today is where the money is going.

HydrogenXT’s expansion positions the United States to remain competitive in the global hydrogen race. Countries across Europe and Asia are investing heavily in hydrogen production, storage, and export capabilities. Developing domestic infrastructure now could help the US secure a stronger foothold in that market.

What comes next?

Construction of the initial facility in California is expected to begin as financing moves into execution. If successful, the first ten plants could serve as a blueprint for broader expansion.

For industries seeking practical pathways to decarbonization, hydrogen offers one piece of the puzzle. For investors, the project represents growing confidence that clean energy infrastructure can deliver both environmental and economic returns.

As 2026 unfolds, deals like this suggest that hydrogen is moving from pilot projects to full-scale deployment, and that the energy transition is entering a new phase of industrial ambition.

This solution is highlighted by The World Business Academy, the umbrella organization housing The Optimist Daily. To learn more, please visit our website.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant $900 million investment to build 10 zero-carbon hydrogen hubs across the US, representing a major step in clean energy infrastructure development. This has broad implications for decarbonizing heavy transport and industry, contributing to climate action and renewable energy transition. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a tangible, large-scale positive impact on society and the environment.
articles.personality_title: HydrogenXT wins $900 million to build 10 zero-carbon hydrogen hubs in the US
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context**
Hydrogen is a clean fuel that can help reduce pollution from trucks and factories. Until recently, most hydrogen came from fossil fuels, which produce carbon emissions. But new technology and investments are changing this.

**What happened**
HydrogenXT, a company based in Houston, secured $900 million to build 10 hydrogen production and refueling centers across the United States. These centers will create and supply zero-carbon hydrogen, meaning they won’t release harmful carbon emissions. The first plant will be in Avenal, California, with others planned in states like Oregon, Washington, and North Dakota.

**Impact**
This project is important because it supports heavy trucks and industries that are hard to run on electricity alone. By producing hydrogen without carbon emissions, these hubs can replace diesel fuel and help reduce pollution. The $900 million investment shows that many people believe hydrogen can be a real and powerful clean energy source.

**What's next step**
Construction of the first hydrogen hub in California will start soon. If successful, the project could grow, creating more hubs and encouraging other companies to invest in clean hydrogen. This could help the US compete globally in hydrogen energy.

**One-sentence takeaway**
HydrogenXT’s $900 million plan to build 10 zero-carbon hydrogen hubs marks a big step toward cleaner fuel for heavy transport and industry in the US.
articles.personality_title_fr: HydrogenXT obtient 900 millions de dollars pour construire 10 centres d’hydrogène zéro carbone aux États-Unis
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte**
L’hydrogène est un carburant propre qui peut aider à réduire la pollution des camions et des usines. Jusqu’à récemment, la plupart de l’hydrogène provenait des combustibles fossiles, qui produisent des émissions de carbone. Mais de nouvelles technologies et investissements changent cela.

**Ce qui s’est passé**
HydrogenXT, une entreprise basée à Houston, a obtenu 900 millions de dollars pour construire 10 centres de production et de ravitaillement en hydrogène à travers les États-Unis. Ces centres produiront et fourniront de l’hydrogène zéro carbone, ce qui signifie qu’ils ne rejetteront pas de gaz nocifs. La première usine sera à Avenal, en Californie, avec d’autres prévues dans des États comme l’Oregon, Washington et le Dakota du Nord.

**Impact**
Ce projet est important car il soutient les camions lourds et les industries difficiles à électrifier. En produisant de l’hydrogène sans émissions de carbone, ces centres peuvent remplacer le diesel et aider à réduire la pollution. L’investissement de 900 millions de dollars montre que beaucoup croient que l’hydrogène peut être une source d’énergie propre réelle et puissante.

**Prochaine étape**
La construction du premier centre d’hydrogène en Californie commencera bientôt. Si le projet réussit, il pourrait s’étendre, créant plus de centres et encourageant d’autres entreprises à investir dans l’hydrogène propre. Cela pourrait aider les États-Unis à être compétitifs au niveau mondial dans l’énergie hydrogène.

**Conclusion en une phrase**
Le plan de 900 millions de dollars d’HydrogenXT pour construire 10 centres d’hydrogène zéro carbone est une avancée majeure vers un carburant plus propre pour le transport lourd et l’industrie aux États-Unis.
articles.personality_title_es: HydrogenXT consigue 900 millones de dólares para construir 10 centros de hidrógeno cero carbono en EE.UU.
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto**
El hidrógeno es un combustible limpio que puede ayudar a reducir la contaminación de camiones y fábricas. Hasta hace poco, la mayoría del hidrógeno provenía de combustibles fósiles, que producen emisiones de carbono. Pero nuevas tecnologías e inversiones están cambiando esto.

**Qué pasó**
HydrogenXT, una empresa con sede en Houston, obtuvo 900 millones de dólares para construir 10 centros de producción y abastecimiento de hidrógeno en todo Estados Unidos. Estos centros crearán y suministrarán hidrógeno cero carbono, lo que significa que no emitirán gases dañinos. La primera planta estará en Avenal, California, y otras en estados como Oregón, Washington y Dakota del Norte.

**Impacto**
Este proyecto es importante porque apoya a los camiones pesados y a industrias que son difíciles de electrificar. Al producir hidrógeno sin emisiones de carbono, estos centros pueden reemplazar el diesel y ayudar a reducir la contaminación. La inversión de 900 millones muestra que muchas personas creen que el hidrógeno puede ser una fuente de energía limpia real y poderosa.

**Próximo paso**
La construcción del primer centro de hidrógeno en California comenzará pronto. Si tiene éxito, el proyecto podría crecer, creando más centros y animando a otras empresas a invertir en hidrógeno limpio. Esto podría ayudar a EE.UU. a competir a nivel mundial en energía de hidrógeno.

**Resumen en una frase**
El plan de HydrogenXT con 900 millones de dólares para construir 10 centros de hidrógeno cero carbono es un gran paso hacia un combustible más limpio para el transporte pesado y la industria en EE.UU.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of ten stylized hydrogen fuel hubs shaped like glowing, interconnected blue and green hexagonal crystals emitting soft light, set along a simplified map outline of the western and midwestern United States with flowing lines symbolizing clean energy corridors connecting them, all under a clear sky with gentle earth tones and subtle hints of industrial shapes like trucks and pipelines abstractly integrated into the landscape.

articles.title: Earth's heat to power 10,000 homes in renewable energy first for UK
articles.summary: Water super-heated by rocks will also provide the UK's first domestic supply of the critical mineral lithium.
articles.content: The further down you go the hotter it gets. At shallow depths the heat is enough to warm our homes and businesses. This is already done in some parts of the UK – for example, ground source heat pumps are a form of geothermal technology.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant renewable energy breakthrough in the UK, using geothermal heat to power 10,000 homes and simultaneously supply lithium, a critical mineral. This has broad environmental and societal benefits by advancing clean energy and resource sustainability.
articles.personality_title: UK to use Earth's heat to power 10,000 homes and mine lithium
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The deeper you go underground, the hotter it gets. Some places in the UK already use this natural heat with ground source heat pumps to warm buildings.

**What happened** – For the first time in the UK, geothermal energy from hot rocks underground will be used to provide power for 10,000 homes. At the same time, this project will extract lithium, an important mineral used in batteries.

**Impact** – This is the UK’s first large-scale use of geothermal heat for electricity and heating combined with lithium mining. It shows how natural heat can be a clean energy source while also supplying valuable materials needed for electric cars and technology.

**What's next step** – The project will help test how geothermal energy and lithium mining can work together. If successful, it could lead to more similar projects across the UK and reduce reliance on fossil fuels and imported minerals.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UK is pioneering a new way to use underground heat to power homes and gather important minerals, advancing renewable energy and resource use.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Royaume-Uni utilisera la chaleur de la Terre pour alimenter 10 000 maisons et extraire du lithium
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Plus on creuse profondément sous terre, plus il fait chaud. Certaines zones du Royaume-Uni utilisent déjà cette chaleur naturelle avec des pompes à chaleur géothermiques pour chauffer les bâtiments.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Pour la première fois au Royaume-Uni, la chaleur géothermique des roches chaudes sera utilisée pour fournir de l’énergie à 10 000 maisons. En même temps, ce projet extraira du lithium, un minéral important utilisé dans les batteries.

**Impact** – C’est la première utilisation à grande échelle de la chaleur géothermique au Royaume-Uni pour l’électricité et le chauffage, combinée à l’extraction de lithium. Cela montre comment la chaleur naturelle peut être une source d’énergie propre tout en fournissant des matériaux précieux pour les voitures électriques et la technologie.

**Prochaine étape** – Le projet permettra de tester comment l’énergie géothermique et l’extraction de lithium peuvent fonctionner ensemble. S’il réussit, cela pourrait conduire à d’autres projets similaires au Royaume-Uni et réduire la dépendance aux combustibles fossiles et aux minéraux importés.

**En une phrase** – Le Royaume-Uni innove en utilisant la chaleur souterraine pour alimenter des maisons et récolter des minéraux importants, faisant avancer les énergies renouvelables et l’utilisation des ressources.
articles.personality_title_es: Reino Unido usará el calor de la Tierra para alimentar 10,000 hogares y extraer litio
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cuanto más profundo se excava bajo tierra, más calor hay. Algunas zonas del Reino Unido ya usan este calor natural con bombas de calor geotérmicas para calentar edificios.

**Qué pasó** – Por primera vez en el Reino Unido, la energía geotérmica de rocas calientes se usará para proveer energía a 10,000 hogares. Al mismo tiempo, este proyecto extraerá litio, un mineral importante para las baterías.

**Impacto** – Esta es la primera vez que el Reino Unido usa a gran escala el calor geotérmico para electricidad y calefacción junto con la minería de litio. Muestra cómo el calor natural puede ser una fuente limpia de energía y también suministrar materiales valiosos para autos eléctricos y tecnología.

**Próximo paso** – El proyecto ayudará a probar cómo pueden funcionar juntos la energía geotérmica y la minería de litio. Si tiene éxito, podría llevar a más proyectos similares en todo el Reino Unido y reducir la dependencia de combustibles fósiles y minerales importados.

**Una frase clave** – El Reino Unido está innovando al usar el calor subterráneo para alimentar hogares y obtener minerales importantes, avanzando en energía renovable y uso de recursos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a cross-section of the Earth’s shallow underground layers glowing with gentle orange and red tones, beneath a peaceful countryside dotted with cozy homes illuminated softly from within, connected by subtle, glowing lines representing ground source heat pumps drawing natural heat upward, all rendered in natural, earthy colors with a calm and hopeful atmosphere.

articles.title: The Earth911 Rain Garden Installation Guide
articles.summary:
When it rains, water runs off your roof, driveway, and sidewalks, picking up fertilizers, oil,...
The post The Earth911 Rain Garden Installation Guide appeared first on Earth911.
articles.content:
When it rains, water runs off your roof, driveway, and sidewalks, picking up fertilizers, oil, pesticides, and sediment before flowing into storm drains and eventually your local waterways. At the same time, treated drinking water is used inside your home to keep your lawn green with sprinklers. This cycle wastes valuable water in both directions. A rain garden is a smart solution. It redirects stormwater runoff into a shallow, planted area where the water filters naturally through soil, recharges groundwater, and nourishes plants without using any extra water.

The EPA reports that outdoor water use accounts for about 30 percent of household water use nationwide, with nearly 9 billion gallons of drinking water used each day for landscaping. In the dry Southwest, outdoor use can reach 60 percent of a household’s total. Up to half of the water used for home irrigation is lost to evaporation, wind, and runoff. Rain gardens help on both fronts. They capture and soak up stormwater, absorbing up to 30 percent more water than a regular lawn, and they also reduce the need for treated water to keep your yard green.

A well-designed rain garden can filter up to 90 percent of nutrient pollutants and 80 percent of sediments from runoff, according to HGTV’s rain garden guide. This means less pollution reaches rivers, lakes, and aquifers, helping protect your community’s drinking water. Whether you live in the rainy Pacific Northwest or the dry West, a rain garden turns a problem into a valuable part of your landscape.

This article includes affiliate links. If you buy something through these links, we earn a small commission that helps support our work.

Why Rain Gardens Matter for Water Conservation

Rain gardens do more than just make your yard look nice. Across the country, homeowners use billions of gallons of treated water to water their lawns and gardens, while letting millions of gallons of free rainwater run off their property and strain city stormwater systems. Rain gardens help break this cycle in several key ways.

Every gallon of rainwater that soaks into your rain garden is a gallon of treated water you don’t need to use for sprinklers. The EPA’s WaterSense program says that nearly 8 billion gallons of water are used each day in the U.S. for outdoor purposes, mostly for landscaping. Most households use more water outside than for showers and laundry combined. By swapping part of your lawn for a rain garden with native plants, you create a landscape that thrives on rain and needs little or no extra watering once it’s established.

The changes you make to use rainwater in the garden will make a difference immediately, because treating water to potable standards requires significant energy. Extracting, purifying, and distributing drinking water accounts for substantial municipal energy expenditures, as the EPA’s Green Infrastructure Municipal Handbook explains. Reducing unnecessary potable water demand for irrigation helps communities conserve both water and the energy required to deliver it.

Recharging Groundwater and Reducing Flooding

Rain gardens use a process called bioretention to slow, capture, and filter stormwater through layers of plants, engineered soil, and sand. According to the University of California’s Agriculture and Natural Resources program, a single rain garden allows approximately 30 percent more water to infiltrate the ground than a conventional lawn, directly replenishing regional underground aquifers. This groundwater recharge supports wells, springs, and base flow in streams—the very sources many communities depend on for drinking water.

In cities and suburbs, hard surfaces like roofs, driveways, and sidewalks stop water from soaking into the ground. The Alliance for the Chesapeake Bay points out that rain gardens help reduce local flooding and recharge groundwater that would otherwise flow into storm drains. For homeowners, this means less standing water in your yard, less erosion, and a garden that puts runoff to good use.

Stormwater runoff from residential neighborhoods also carries a cocktail of pollutants: fertilizer nutrients, pesticides, motor oil from driveways, bacteria from pet waste, and sediment from eroding soil. The plants, soil microorganisms, and engineered media in a rain garden work together as a biological filter. The Virginia Tech Extension explains that these bioretention systems clean runoff before it flows to storm drains, preventing pollution from reaching local waterways. This protection extends to the drinking water sources that downstream communities depend upon.

Rain Garden Design Inspiration

A rain garden doesn’t have to look like a drainage ditch. Today’s best designs integrate seamlessly into residential landscapes, so a colorful rain garden doesn’t have to look like a ditch. The best designs fit right into your yard as colorful flower beds, natural meadows, or eye-catching features.

Garden designer and author Benjamin Vogt of Prairie Up has become one of the most influential voices in naturalistic, native-plant landscape design. His approach to rain gardens emphasizes planting communities that mimic local ecosystems—combining deep-rooted prairie grasses with native wildflowers such as purple coneflower, black-eyed Susan, and blue flag iris — to create a landscape that manages stormwater while supporting pollinators and wildlife. Vogt’s blog, The Deep Middle, offers a wealth of design philosophy and practical guidance for turning suburban lawns into functional, beautiful ecosystems. His book Prairie Up: An Introduction to Natural Garden Design provides step-by-step blueprints for prairie-inspired plantings adaptable to rain garden settings.

Garden Design highlights an approach by landscape architect Jim Hagstrom of Savanna Designs: make your rain garden part of your whole yard, not just a separate feature. Think about the garden’s shape, plant choices, and borders so they fit with your home and other garden beds. Using paver edging gives a neat look, while river rocks and grasses create a more relaxed, meadow feel. The main idea is that your rain garden should look like it fits in.

Sunset magazine featured a design by Sophie Pennes of Urban Farms LA, who creates rain capture systems for homes in Southern California. She shows how rain gardens can replace thirsty front lawns by using a planted depression in the ground that collects runoff from roofs and driveways, creating seasonal ecosystems. In dry areas, this approach is especially useful because it reduces or eliminates the need for supplemental irrigation and makes your yard more interesting whenever it rains.

HGTV’s rain garden gallery highlights designs that deliver year-round visual interest by combining summer-blooming perennials like gold black-eyed Susan and purple Russian sage with ornamental grasses that provide winter structure. One standout approach is a rain garden with a footbridge spanning the basin, transforming an eco-friendly infrastructure feature into a landscape showpiece. Plantings include creeping thyme on the upper edges and moisture-loving sedges and fescues in the basin.

How to Install a Rain Garden

Most homeowners can install a rain garden over a weekend. The steps include picking the right spot, figuring out the size, digging, improving the soil, and planting. Here’s a step-by-step guide with the tools and materials you’ll need.

Step 1: Choose Your Site and Call Before You Dig

Find a spot that naturally collects runoff from your roof, driveway, or other hard surfaces. The best place is at least 10 feet from your home’s foundation to avoid moisture issues and should be downhill from a downspout or paved area. Stay away from spots over utility lines, near septic systems, or under big trees with lots of roots. Before digging, call 811 or your local utility service to mark underground lines.

What you’ll need: To assess your site, grab a 25-foot tape measure and landscape marking flags to lay out the garden’s perimeter. A line level or a builder’s level will help you assess slope.

Step 2: Conduct a Soil Percolation Test

Your rain garden needs well-draining soil. Dig a hole about 12 inches deep, fill it with water, and let it drain. Fill it again and time how long it takes to drain. Aim for about one inch per hour. If the soil drains too slowly, dig deeper and add more soil mix. If it drains too fast, add organic matter to help plants grow.

What you’ll need: A post-hole digger or garden auger drill bit for digging the test hole, a ruler or yardstick, and a timer to measure infiltration rate.

Step 3: Size Your Rain Garden

A good rule is to make your rain garden about 10 to 30 percent of the size of the area that drains into it. For example, if 500 square feet of roof drains to the garden and you have loamy soil, plan for a 100-square-foot garden. The garden should be 6 to 12 inches deep with a flat bottom for even water spread.

Step 4: Excavate and Shape the Basin

Mark the perimeter with stakes and string, following natural contours for an organic shape. Excavate the basin to a depth of 18 to 24 inches (accounting for 12 to 18 inches of amended soil mix plus 6 inches of ponding depth). Create gently sloped sides and a flat bottom. Pile the excavated subsoil on the downhill side to form a berm, a compacted mound that keeps water in the garden. The berm should be level across the top so water doesn’t find a low point and erode through.

What you’ll need: A flat-blade shovel and a garden spade for excavation. A wheelbarrow makes moving soil much easier. For layout, use wooden stakes and a mason’s line. A garden rake will help you smooth and level the basin floor.

Step 5: Prepare the Soil Mix

Rain garden soil is specially mixed for good drainage and healthy plants. The usual mix is about 60 percent sand, 20 percent compost, and 20 percent topsoil. This blend lets water soak in quickly but keeps enough moisture and nutrients for plants between rains. Fill the basin with 12 to 18 inches of this mix.

What you’ll need: For a DIY mix, source coarse builder’s sand, organic compost, and clean topsoil from your local landscape supplier (buying in bulk from a nearby yard will be more economical than bagged products for large volumes). For smaller gardens, bagged garden soil amendments and perlite can improve drainage and water retention in existing soil. A soil pH test kit will help you ensure your mix falls within the slightly acidic to neutral pH range (5.5–7.0) that most native plants prefer.

Step 6: Install Inlet and Overflow Structures

Connect your rain garden to its water source. If fed by a downspout, run a corrugated drainpipe or a flexible downspout extension from the downspout to the garden, with a gentle slope. At the entry point, place a layer of river rock or drain rock to form a splash pad that dissipates water energy and prevents erosion at the inlet. On the opposite side, create an overflow point—a low spot in the berm armored with stone—where excess water can safely exit during heavy storms. For additional overflow management, a channel drain can direct surplus water away from foundations.

Step 7: Plant in Zones

Rain garden plants fall into three zones based on moisture tolerance. In the deepest part of the basin (Zone 1), plant species that tolerate periodic standing water include blue flag iris, cardinal flower, swamp milkweed, fox sedge, and great blue lobelia. On the garden’s sloped sides (Zone 2), choose plants that handle alternating wet and dry conditions: black-eyed Susan, bee balm, switchgrass, and New England aster. Along the upper edges and berm (Zone 3), select drought-tolerant species: purple coneflower, little bluestem, prairie dropseed, and butterfly weed.

What you’ll need: Source native plants from a local nursery whenever possible for the best regional adaptation. A garden trowel set and gardening gloves are essential for planting. Use hardwood mulch to cover exposed soil between plants—apply 2 to 3 inches, keeping mulch a few inches away from plant stems. A layer of mulch suppresses weeds, retains soil moisture, and prevents erosion as your plants establish. Avoid dyed mulch and use natural, undyed hardwood or shredded leaf mulch.

Step 8: Water, Maintain, and Observe

Water your new rain garden regularly, about an inch per week, during the first growing season to help plants grow deep roots. After the first year, your rain garden should mostly take care of itself and need little extra watering. This is when you really start saving water. Maintenance is similar to a regular garden bed: weed during the first year, add mulch each year, and clear out debris that could block the inlet. After storms, check that water drains within 24 to 48 hours. If it takes longer, add more sand to the soil.

What you’ll need: A rain gauge helps you track rainfall and know when supplemental watering is necessary during establishment. A soaker hose connected to a rain barrel provides the ultimate closed-loop water system that captures additional rooftop runoff for your garden’s establishment irrigation without touching your potable water supply.

Check for Local Incentives

Many municipalities offer financial incentives for installing rain gardens. Some cities and counties offer rebates, reduced stormwater utility fees, or cost-sharing programs for homeowners who install green stormwater infrastructure. The Virginia Tech Extension recommends contacting your local stormwater, public works, or soil and water conservation office to find out about available programs. The EPA’s Soak Up the Rain initiative also provides state-by-state resources, design guides, and technical support for residential rain garden projects.

Make Every Drop Count

A rain garden is one of the best things you can do at home to save water. By capturing and soaking up stormwater on your property, you use less treated water, help refill local groundwater, filter out pollutants before they reach waterways, and create a home for pollinators and wildlife. Plus, you get a beautiful, low-maintenance yard feature.

As communities face old water systems, higher utility costs, and the effects of climate change on water supplies, the simple rain garden is an easy, scalable part of the solution. One garden at a time, we can keep clean water where it belongs and let the rain do the job our sprinklers once did.
articles.reasoning: The article describes rain gardens as an effective, scalable environmental solution that conserves water, reduces pollution, recharges groundwater, and mitigates flooding. It provides detailed, practical guidance for homeowners to implement rain gardens, which have broad significance for communities facing water scarcity and pollution challenges. The story focuses on a single impactful topic with substantial context and actionable information.
articles.personality_title: How rain gardens help save water and protect local waterways
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – When it rains, water runs off roofs, driveways, and sidewalks, carrying pollution into storm drains and local rivers. At the same time, many people use treated drinking water to keep their lawns green, which wastes valuable water.

**What happened** – Rain gardens are planted areas designed to catch and soak up rainwater from hard surfaces. This water filters naturally through soil, recharges underground water supplies, and nourishes plants without using extra water. Homeowners can build rain gardens by choosing a spot, digging a basin, improving the soil, and planting native plants that handle wet and dry conditions.

**Impact** – Rain gardens reduce the need for treated water in watering lawns by using free rainwater instead. They can absorb up to 30% more water than regular lawns and filter out up to 90% of harmful nutrients and 80% of sediments from runoff. This helps protect drinking water sources and reduces flooding and pollution in local waterways.

**What's next step** – Many cities offer financial help to homeowners who build rain gardens. People can contact local offices or use online resources to learn about programs and get advice. As more rain gardens are installed, communities can better manage water and protect the environment.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Rain gardens are simple, effective gardens that save treated water, reduce pollution, and help recharge groundwater by using rainwater where it falls.
articles.personality_title_fr: Comment les jardins de pluie aident à économiser l'eau et protéger les cours d'eau locaux
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Quand il pleut, l'eau ruisselle des toits, des allées et des trottoirs, emportant des polluants vers les égouts pluviaux et les rivières locales. En même temps, beaucoup utilisent de l'eau potable traitée pour arroser leurs pelouses, ce qui gaspille de l'eau précieuse.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Les jardins de pluie sont des espaces plantés conçus pour capter et absorber l'eau de pluie provenant des surfaces dures. Cette eau filtre naturellement à travers le sol, recharge les nappes souterraines et nourrit les plantes sans utiliser d'eau supplémentaire. Les propriétaires peuvent créer un jardin de pluie en choisissant un emplacement, en creusant une cuvette, en améliorant le sol et en plantant des plantes locales adaptées aux conditions humides et sèches.

**Impact** – Les jardins de pluie réduisent le besoin d'eau potable pour arroser les pelouses en utilisant l'eau de pluie gratuite. Ils peuvent absorber jusqu'à 30 % d'eau de plus qu'une pelouse normale et filtrer jusqu'à 90 % des nutriments nocifs et 80 % des sédiments du ruissellement. Cela aide à protéger les sources d'eau potable et à réduire les inondations et la pollution des cours d'eau locaux.

**Prochaine étape** – De nombreuses villes offrent une aide financière aux propriétaires qui installent des jardins de pluie. Les gens peuvent contacter les bureaux locaux ou utiliser des ressources en ligne pour connaître les programmes et obtenir des conseils. Au fur et à mesure que davantage de jardins de pluie sont installés, les communautés peuvent mieux gérer l'eau et protéger l'environnement.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les jardins de pluie sont des jardins simples et efficaces qui économisent l'eau potable, réduisent la pollution et aident à recharger les nappes phréatiques en utilisant l'eau de pluie sur place.
articles.personality_title_es: Cómo los jardines de lluvia ayudan a ahorrar agua y proteger los ríos locales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cuando llueve, el agua corre por los techos, entradas y aceras, llevando contaminantes a los desagües pluviales y ríos locales. Al mismo tiempo, muchas personas usan agua potable tratada para regar sus jardines, lo que desperdicia agua valiosa.

**Qué pasó** – Los jardines de lluvia son áreas plantadas diseñadas para captar y absorber el agua de lluvia de superficies duras. Esta agua se filtra naturalmente a través del suelo, recarga el agua subterránea y alimenta las plantas sin usar agua extra. Los propietarios pueden construir jardines de lluvia eligiendo un lugar, cavando una cuenca, mejorando el suelo y plantando plantas nativas que toleran condiciones húmedas y secas.

**Impacto** – Los jardines de lluvia reducen la necesidad de usar agua potable para regar al aprovechar el agua de lluvia gratuita. Pueden absorber hasta un 30 % más de agua que el césped común y filtrar hasta el 90 % de nutrientes dañinos y el 80 % de sedimentos del agua de escorrentía. Esto ayuda a proteger las fuentes de agua potable y reduce inundaciones y contaminación en los ríos locales.

**Próximo paso** – Muchas ciudades ofrecen ayuda económica a los propietarios que instalan jardines de lluvia. Las personas pueden contactar oficinas locales o usar recursos en línea para conocer programas y recibir asesoría. A medida que se instalen más jardines de lluvia, las comunidades podrán manejar mejor el agua y proteger el medio ambiente.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Los jardines de lluvia son jardines simples y efectivos que ahorran agua potable, reducen la contaminación y ayudan a recargar el agua subterránea usando el agua de lluvia donde cae.
articles.image_prompt: A serene, gently contoured garden basin filled with layered soil and native prairie plants like purple coneflowers, black-eyed Susans, and blue flag iris, showing clear water slowly soaking into the earth beneath soft rain clouds, with a subtle flow of clean water moving away from a rooftop downspout toward the garden, all rendered in warm, earthy tones with delicate brushstrokes capturing the harmony of natural water filtration and groundwater recharge.

articles.title: Classic Sustainability In Your Ear: The Ocean River Institute’s Natural Lawn Challenge for Climate Action
articles.summary:
Turn back the clock with this classic interview that will get you ready for Spring...
The post Classic Sustainability In Your Ear: The Ocean River Institute’s Natural Lawn Challenge for Climate Action appeared first on Earth911.
articles.content:
Turn back the clock with this classic interview that will get you ready for Spring yard care planning. A lawn may be beautiful but it can take a heavy toll on the environment, accounting for between 30% and 60% of residential water use in the United States. Rob Moir, Ph.D., is president and executive director of the Ocean River Institute in Cambridge, Massachusetts. ORI works with residential lawn owners to heal damaged ecosystems by restoring coastal areas to lessen the destructive impacts of climate change. The benefits of a natural lawn reach far beyond reduced local water pollution, eliminating chemicals that can contribute to cancers, diabetes, Alzheimer’s disease, and other cellular diseases. Natural lawns are also better for local pollinators and store much more carbon than heavily fertilized lawns. If you considered removing your lawn to play a part in the battle against climate change, this interview may change your mind — a healthy lawn is a powerful carbon sink.

The Ocean River Institute is recruiting Massachusetts communities, town by town, to take a pledge to follow natural lawn practices in the Healthy Soils for Climate Restoration Challenge. You don’t need to live in Massachusetts to participate and learn about the alternatives to the traditional, chemical-intensive lawn practices that use Roundup, a source of glyphosates that kills soil-dwelling fungi and local pollinators, and fast-acting nitrogen fertilizers. You can learn more about the Ocean River Institute at www.oceanriver.org.

Rob has contributed many articles about climate change and the history of environmental change since this interview, including:

Subscribe to Sustainability in Your Ear on iTunes and Apple Podcasts.

Follow Sustainability in Your Ear on Spreaker, iHeartRadio, or YouTube

Editor’s Note: This episode originally aired on May 30, 2022.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a meaningful environmental initiative by the Ocean River Institute promoting natural lawn care to reduce water use, chemical pollution, and enhance carbon sequestration, benefiting climate action and local ecosystems. It presents a clear, impactful program with broad relevance beyond a niche audience.
articles.personality_title: Ocean River Institute launches natural lawn challenge to protect climate and local wildlife
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many people have lawns at home, but keeping them green often uses a lot of water and chemicals that can harm the environment. In the United States, lawns can use between 30% and 60% of water in a neighborhood. 

**What happened** – The Ocean River Institute (ORI), based in Cambridge, Massachusetts, started a program called the Healthy Soils for Climate Restoration Challenge. Led by Dr. Rob Moir, ORI encourages people and towns to care for their lawns naturally, without harmful chemicals like Roundup and strong fertilizers. Instead of removing lawns, they promote healthy natural lawns that support the soil and local animals like bees.

**Impact** – This is important because natural lawns help store carbon, which slows climate change. They also reduce pollution in local water and protect pollinators that are vital for plants and food. Using fewer chemicals can lessen health risks linked to those substances. The program offers a way for communities to work together on climate action by changing how they care for their yards.

**What's next step** – ORI is inviting more towns in Massachusetts and beyond to join the challenge and learn about natural lawn care. People can visit their website to find out how to participate and make a difference in their own yards.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The Ocean River Institute’s natural lawn challenge helps communities protect the climate and wildlife by promoting healthy, chemical-free lawns that save water and store carbon.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’Institut Ocean River lance un défi pour des pelouses naturelles qui protègent le climat et la faune locale
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Beaucoup de personnes ont une pelouse chez elles, mais en prendre soin demande souvent beaucoup d’eau et des produits chimiques qui peuvent nuire à l’environnement. Aux États-Unis, les pelouses peuvent utiliser entre 30 % et 60 % de l’eau d’un quartier.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – L’Institut Ocean River (ORI), basé à Cambridge dans le Massachusetts, a lancé un programme appelé le Défi des sols sains pour la restauration du climat. Dirigé par le Dr Rob Moir, ORI encourage les habitants et les villes à entretenir leurs pelouses de façon naturelle, sans produits chimiques nocifs comme le Roundup ou les engrais puissants. Plutôt que d’enlever les pelouses, ils promeuvent des pelouses naturelles saines qui soutiennent le sol et les animaux locaux comme les abeilles.

**Impact** – Cela est important parce que les pelouses naturelles aident à stocker le carbone, ce qui ralentit le changement climatique. Elles réduisent aussi la pollution de l’eau locale et protègent les pollinisateurs essentiels pour les plantes et la nourriture. Utiliser moins de produits chimiques diminue aussi les risques pour la santé liés à ces substances. Ce programme offre un moyen aux communautés d’agir ensemble pour le climat en changeant leur façon d’entretenir leurs jardins.

**Prochaine étape** – L’ORI invite plus de villes dans le Massachusetts et ailleurs à rejoindre le défi et à découvrir comment entretenir leur pelouse naturellement. Les gens peuvent visiter leur site web pour savoir comment participer et agir dans leur propre jardin.

**Phrase clé** – Le défi de pelouse naturelle de l’Institut Ocean River aide les communautés à protéger le climat et la faune en promouvant des pelouses saines sans produits chimiques qui économisent l’eau et stockent le carbone.
articles.personality_title_es: El Instituto Ocean River lanza un desafío de césped natural para proteger el clima y la fauna local
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas personas tienen césped en casa, pero cuidarlo a menudo usa mucha agua y productos químicos que pueden dañar el medio ambiente. En Estados Unidos, el césped puede usar entre el 30 % y el 60 % del agua de un vecindario.

**Qué pasó** – El Instituto Ocean River (ORI), con sede en Cambridge, Massachusetts, inició un programa llamado Desafío de Suelos Saludables para la Restauración del Clima. Dirigido por el Dr. Rob Moir, ORI anima a las personas y pueblos a cuidar sus céspedes de manera natural, sin químicos dañinos como Roundup ni fertilizantes fuertes. En lugar de quitar el césped, promueven céspedes naturales saludables que apoyan el suelo y animales locales como las abejas.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque los céspedes naturales ayudan a almacenar carbono, lo que frena el cambio climático. También reducen la contaminación del agua local y protegen a los polinizadores necesarios para las plantas y los alimentos. Usar menos químicos puede reducir riesgos para la salud relacionados con esas sustancias. El programa ofrece una forma para que las comunidades actúen juntas en la lucha contra el cambio climático cambiando cómo cuidan sus jardines.

**Próximo paso** – ORI invita a más pueblos de Massachusetts y otros lugares a unirse al desafío y aprender sobre el cuidado natural del césped. Las personas pueden visitar su sitio web para saber cómo participar y marcar la diferencia en sus propios jardines.

**Frase clave** – El desafío de césped natural del Instituto Ocean River ayuda a las comunidades a proteger el clima y la fauna promoviendo céspedes saludables sin químicos que ahorran agua y almacenan carbono.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a vibrant, lush natural lawn teeming with healthy soil layers visible beneath, showing diverse roots and earthworms, surrounded by delicate native pollinator insects gently hovering, with a flowing river in the background symbolizing the Ocean River Institute’s coastal restoration efforts, all rendered in soft, natural earth tones and greens.
